county_metadata.record_losses: 1882 and 1914 courthouse fires destroyed significant early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jasper County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Bay Springs. Burned county: 1882 and 1914 courthouse fires destroyed significant early records. Two judicial districts (Bay Springs and Paulding). Named for Sergeant William Jasper of the Revolutionary War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jasper County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) is a burned county due to 1882 and 1914 courthouse fires that destroyed significant early records. County seat is Bay Springs (the county has two judicial districts: Bay Springs and Paulding). The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. For the burned periods, use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm of any surviving fragments, and census records. Jasper County is in the Piney Woods with a mixed economy of timber and farming. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. Church records from Baptist congregations are important supplements. FamilySearch has some Jasper County records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps in the destroyed record base.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Jasper County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Bay Springs maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1799 as one of Mississippi Territory's two original counties (with Adams). Originally named Pickering County, renamed 1802. County seat is Fayette. Among the oldest counties in Mississippi with records from the territorial period.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County (formed 1799 as one of Mississippi's original two counties) has Fayette as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1798 and chancery court records, making Jefferson County's records among the oldest in the state. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Jefferson County records. The county was a major slaveholding center in the Natchez District -- estate inventories and plantation records at MDAH document large enslaved populations. Spanish colonial records for the area are in the Mississippi Provincial Archives at MDAH. Freedmen's Bureau records for southwest Mississippi are essential for post-war African American research. BLM GLO land patents document early federal entries. Church records from Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian congregations dating from the early 1800s are at MDAH and local churches. FamilySearch has digitized Jefferson County chancery records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Jefferson County deed books from 1799; Chancery Clerk in Fayette maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson Davis County formed 1906 from Covington and Lawrence counties. County seat is Prentiss. Located in the Piney Woods of south Mississippi. Named for Confederate President Jefferson Davis.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson Davis County (formed 1906 from Covington and Lawrence) has Prentiss as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1906. For pre-1906 records, check Covington County (Collins -- note the 1872 courthouse fire) and Lawrence County (Monticello). MDAH has microfilm of Jefferson Davis County records. The county is in the Piney Woods region with a timber and farming economy. FamilySearch has some Jefferson Davis County clerk records. Baptist church records are important sources for vital events. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. Census records from 1910 forward capture the new county; earlier census data is under Covington and Lawrence.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Jefferson Davis County deed books from 1906; Chancery Clerk in Prentiss maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1884 courthouse fire destroyed many early records; Jones County was notable for Unionist sentiment during Civil War (Free State of Jones)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jones County formed 1826 from Covington and Wayne counties. County seat is Ellisville (Laurel is the larger city). Burned county: 1884 courthouse fire. Historically significant for the 'Free State of Jones' -- Unionist dissent during the Civil War led by Newton Knight.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jones County (formed 1826 from Covington and Wayne) is a burned county due to the 1884 courthouse fire. County seat is Ellisville (Laurel is the larger city). Jones County is historically significant for the 'Free State of Jones' -- Unionist dissent during the Civil War led by Newton Knight. The Chancery Clerk holds reconstructed and post-fire records. For the burned period, use BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), parent county records, and MDAH microfilm. The Lauren Rogers Museum of Art in Laurel holds some local history materials. The timber industry transformed Jones County in the late 1800s. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has some Jones County records. Victoria Bynum's research on the Free State of Jones provides scholarly context. Mixed-race Knight community records are at MDAH and in published genealogies.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Jones County deed books from 1826; Chancery Clerk in Ellisville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kemper County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is DeKalb. Located on the Alabama border in east Mississippi. Named for Reuben Kemper, who participated in the West Florida Rebellion.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kemper County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has DeKalb as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Kemper County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. Kemper County is on the Alabama border and was settled by migrants from Alabama, Georgia, and the Carolinas. BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document Choctaw Cession entries. The county had a significant enslaved population on cotton plantations. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. Some Choctaw families remained in Kemper County after removal -- NARA Record Group 75 holds relevant records. FamilySearch has digitized Kemper County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s-1840s document early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Kemper County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in DeKalb maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 Union troops burned courthouse and much of Oxford; significant Civil War record losses
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lafayette County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Oxford, home to the University of Mississippi. Burned county: 1864 Union troops burned the courthouse and much of Oxford. Named for the Marquis de Lafayette. William Faulkner's literary 'Yoknapatawpha County' is based on Lafayette.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lafayette County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) is a burned county because Union troops burned the courthouse and much of Oxford in 1864. County seat is Oxford, home to the University of Mississippi (Ole Miss). The Chancery Clerk holds reconstructed and post-war records. For the burned period, use BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm, and census records. The University of Mississippi Archives and Special Collections hold important regional history materials including the L.Q.C. Lamar papers and plantation records. William Faulkner's 'Yoknapatawpha County' is based on Lafayette County. MDAH has microfilm of surviving and reconstructed Lafayette County records. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Oxford sub-district document African American families. FamilySearch has digitized available Lafayette County clerk records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lafayette County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Oxford maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lamar County formed 1904 from Marion and Pearl River counties. County seat is Purvis. Located in south Mississippi in the Piney Woods. Named for Lucius Q.C. Lamar, US Supreme Court Justice from Mississippi. Camp Shelby military training facility is partly within the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lamar County (formed 1904 from Marion and Pearl River) has Purvis as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1904. For pre-1904 records, check Marion County (Columbia -- note the 1881 fire) and Pearl River County (Poplarville). MDAH has microfilm of Lamar County records. The county is in the Piney Woods and was historically a timber economy. Hattiesburg (in neighboring Forrest County) influenced Lamar County development. FamilySearch has some Lamar County records. Baptist church records are the primary denominational source. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. Camp Shelby, established in 1917 as a WWI training camp, is partly in Lamar County -- military personnel records at NARA may be relevant.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lamar County deed books from 1904; Chancery Clerk in Purvis maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lauderdale County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Meridian, a major railroad junction city burned by Sherman in 1864 but rebuilt rapidly. Named for Colonel James Lauderdale, killed at the Battle of New Orleans.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lauderdale County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Meridian as county seat, the second largest city in Mississippi during the late 1800s. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Lauderdale County land records, chancery minutes, and marriage books. Meridian was a major railroad junction and was burned by Sherman's forces in 1864, though the courthouse records were largely saved. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The Lauderdale County Department of Archives and History in Meridian holds local collections. Meridian had a significant Jewish community -- Temple Beth Israel records are important. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Meridian sub-district are extensive. FamilySearch has digitized Lauderdale County records. City directories for Meridian from the 1880s are at MDAH.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lauderdale County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Meridian maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lawrence County formed 1814 from Marion County. County seat is Monticello. Located in south-central Mississippi along the Natchez Trace corridor. Named for Captain James Lawrence ('Don't give up the ship').
county_metadata.research_tips: Lawrence County (formed 1814 from Marion) has Monticello as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1814 and chancery court records. Lawrence County has well-preserved early records. MDAH has microfilm of Lawrence County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document early federal entries. Lawrence County is in the transition zone between the Piney Woods and river counties. Enslaved persons records, including estate inventories, are at MDAH. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. Baptist church records from the 1810s-1820s are important early sources. FamilySearch has digitized Lawrence County chancery records. Early settlers migrated from Georgia, the Carolinas, and Virginia via the Natchez Trace.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Lawrence County deed books from 1814; Chancery Clerk in Monticello maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson

source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally good survival at MDAH

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Lawrence County churches, community cemeteries, and family burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; MSGenWeb volunteers have transcribed many Lawrence County cemeteries; particularly valuable when civil records are incomplete or destroyed

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Leake County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Carthage. Adjacent to the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians reservation. Named for Governor Walter Leake.
county_metadata.research_tips: Leake County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Carthage as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Leake County records. Leake County is adjacent to the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians reservation, and Choctaw families who remained after removal are documented in NARA Record Group 75. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The county has a mixed economy of farming and timber. The Neshoba County Fair (nearby) historically drew families from Leake County. FamilySearch has digitized Leake County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s document early families. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding patterns from the county's formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Leake County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Carthage maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov
